credit_headline.narrative: Bank credit hit ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, growing 17.7% year-on-year — the fastest pace on record and a decisive re-acceleration after April's post-year-end dip. The system added ₹31.2L Cr in FY26, the largest annual deployment tracked, with non-food credit (₹213.8L Cr) driving 99% of the expansion. All four main sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — are growing simultaneously for the ninth straight period, signaling synchronized demand across the economy. Lenders face a high-growth, high-competition environment: expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality as the credit cycle sustains this aggressive pace into FY27.

sibc-bank-credit-abs.title: Bank credit at ₹215.2L Cr — new all-time high
sibc-bank-credit-abs.observation: Total bank credit outstanding reached ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, the highest level on record.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹3.0L Cr period-on-period (₹212.1L Cr → ₹215.2L Cr), reversing April's post-year-end dip and resuming the climb from ₹178.7L Cr in January 2025.
chain: Bank credit hit ₹215.2L Cr in May 2026, up ₹3.0L Cr from April's ₹212.1L Cr.
April's decline was a temporary correction; May's rebound confirms the underlying expansion trajectory remains intact.
The system has added ₹36.5L Cr since January 2025, with no sign of structural slowdown.
sibc-bank-credit-abs.inference: The credit cycle is back on track. Lenders should prepare for sustained high-volume deployment through FY27 — this is a growth environment, not a consolidation phase. Watch for capacity constraints and asset quality pressure as the system scales.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.title: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25, 17.1% in FY26 — 7.1pp step-up
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.observation: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year growth rate jumped from 10.0% in the year ending March 2025 to 17.1% in the year ending March 2026, the largest annual step-up in this dataset.
chain: Bank credit grew 10.0% in FY25 and 17.1% in FY26, a 7.1 percentage point acceleration.
This is the sharpest year-over-year step-up recorded, signaling a decisive shift in the credit cycle.
Lenders that scaled underwriting and distribution in FY25 are now capturing compounding gains in FY26.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-acceleration.inference: The credit cycle has turned decisively. Banks and NBFCs that built capacity early are winning share; laggards face permanent disadvantage. Expect FY27 to sustain this pace unless macro headwinds emerge.

sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.title: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr in FY26 — record annual add
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.observation: Banks deployed ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the highest annual addition in this window.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.direction: This figure has remained constant at ₹31.2L Cr across all nine periods, as it reflects the full-year tally locked at March 2026 year-end.
chain: Banks added ₹31.2L Cr of net new credit in FY26, the largest single-year deployment on record.
This volume exceeds any prior annual addition tracked, signaling aggressive system-wide expansion.
Growth at this velocity can mask emerging stress in weaker pockets or over-leveraged segments.
sibc-bank-credit-fy-delta.inference: The system added more credit in one year than in any prior period tracked. Lenders must audit asset quality and capital adequacy headroom — growth at this scale demands heightened risk discipline.

sibc-bank-credit-yoy.title: Bank credit YoY at 17.7% — re-accelerating to record high
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.observation: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, the highest growth rate in this dataset.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated 1.6 percentage points (16.0% → 17.7%), reversing April's post-year-end slowdown and surpassing March's 17.1% peak.
chain: Bank credit grew 17.7% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 16.0% in April 2026.
April's deceleration was a temporary normalization; May's re-acceleration confirms the underlying momentum is intact.
Growth is now at the highest level recorded in this nine-period window, above the 16.5% upper range threshold.
sibc-bank-credit-yoy.inference: April's slowdown was noise, not signal. Credit is expanding faster than at any point in this dataset. Lenders face intense competition for quality borrowers — expect pricing pressure, margin compression, and heightened underwriting discipline to sustain portfolio quality.

sibc-food-credit-abs.direction: Volume rose ₹0.33L Cr period-on-period (₹1.04L Cr → ₹1.37L Cr), the second consecutive large increase and a new all-time high.
chain: Food credit hit ₹1.37L Cr in May 2026, up ₹0.33L Cr from April's ₹1.04L Cr.

Food credit remains a small fraction of the ₹215L Cr system, but the surge signals active procurement financing.
sibc-food-credit-abs.inference: Food credit is a rounding error in the system, but the two-period surge signals active procurement financing. Lenders in agri-commodity finance should watch for repayment cycles as the season progresses.

sibc-food-credit-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated 27.7 percentage points (66.4% → 94.1%), reversing April's sharp deceleration and returning to the upper range near February's 94.2% peak.

sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.title: All 4 main sectors growing — breadth at maximum
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.observation: All four main credit sectors — agriculture, industry, services, retail — posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026.
sibc-sectors-positive-yoy-count.direction: Breadth has held at 4 out of 4 sectors for the entire nine-period window, with no sector slipping into contraction.
chain: All four main credit sectors posted positive year-on-year growth in May 2026, maintaining full breadth.

psl.narrative: Priority sector lending is undergoing a structural shift: Social Infrastructure exploded to 70.7% YoY, Housing held at 39.0%, and five segments changed status in a single period — the broadest momentum shift in this dataset. The 81.9pp spread between top and bottom performers is the widest on record, driven by retail and infrastructure categories pulling away while Export Credit and Others remain in contraction (though moderating). Banks are concentrating PSL deployment in consumer-facing segments — housing, education, social infrastructure — and mid-tier enterprises, abandoning export-linked and legacy corporate categories. For lenders, PSL compliance now means overweighting infrastructure and retail; for borrowers in Micro/Small and Medium Enterprises, access is improving as those segments shift to active and strengthening status.

sibc-psl-agri-yoy.title: PSL Agriculture 13.8% YoY — 3.5pp deceleration from 17.3%
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.observation: PSL Agriculture credit grew 13.84% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 3.49pp from 17.3% → 13.8%, breaking a six-period acceleration from 11.0% in Mar 2025.
chain: Agriculture credit grew 13.84% YoY in May 2026, down from 17.33% in Apr 2026 — a 3.49pp deceleration.
This breaks a six-period climb from 11.0% in Mar 2025 to 17.3% in Apr 2026, the first meaningful pullback since the uptrend began.
The deceleration suggests the kharif-driven surge may be moderating, though growth remains above the historical median of 11.6%.
sibc-psl-agri-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending remains healthy at 13.8%, but the sharp deceleration from 17.3% signals the peak may have passed. Banks should monitor whether this is a seasonal correction or the start of a structural slowdown — hold current exposure but pause aggressive expansion until the trend stabilises.

sibc-psl-education-yoy.title: PSL Education Loans 22.6% YoY — stable near upper range
sibc-psl-education-yoy.observation: PSL Educational Loans grew 22.59% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 23.27% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.direction: Growth eased 0.68pp from 23.3% → 22.6%, holding in the 22–24% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
chain: Education loans grew 22.59% YoY in May 2026, down 0.68pp from 23.27% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 22–24% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 22.6% since the breakout from near-zero growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at high growth confirms this is a structural re-rating, not a cyclical spike.
sibc-psl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending is locked in at 22–24% growth — a structural opportunity. Banks should continue scaling origination capacity and expect sustained demand through the academic cycle. The 0.68pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

sibc-psl-export-yoy.title: PSL Export Credit -9.7% YoY — contraction easing from -12.0%
sibc-psl-export-yoy.observation: PSL Export Credit contracted 9.66% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.direction: The contraction narrowed 2.36pp from -12.0% → -9.7%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -8.4% in Mar 2026.
chain: Export credit contracted 9.66% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.02% in Apr 2026 — a 2.36pp narrowing.
This resumes the easing trend from -17.2% in Jan 2026 → -8.8% in Mar 2026, after Apr's reversal to -12.0%.
The contraction remains near the historical median of -13.9%, signaling persistent weakness in trade finance.
sibc-psl-export-yoy.inference: Export credit is stabilising but not yet recovering — the move from -12.0% to -9.7% is progress, but growth remains negative. Banks should stay cautious on export lending until the contraction turns positive for at least two consecutive periods.

sibc-psl-housing-yoy.title: PSL Housing 39.0% YoY — holding near 40% for six periods
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.observation: PSL Housing credit grew 38.98% year-on-year in May 2026, down marginally from 39.94% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.direction: Growth eased 0.96pp from 39.9% → 39.0%, holding in the 37–40% band for six straight periods since Dec 2025.
chain: Housing credit grew 38.98% YoY in May 2026, down 0.96pp from 39.94% in Apr 2026.
Growth has held in the 37–40% range for six consecutive periods, with no reading below 36.9% since the breakout from negative growth in Mar 2025.
The stability at near-40% growth confirms this is the structural engine of PSL expansion.
sibc-psl-housing-yoy.inference: Housing is the anchor of PSL growth at 39.0% — demand is structural, not cyclical. Banks should maximise exposure to this segment and expect sustained deployment through FY27. The 0.96pp dip is noise within a stable high-growth regime.

sibc-psl-medium-yoy.title: PSL Medium Enterprises 17.8% YoY — reversing deceleration trend
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.observation: PSL Medium Enterprises credit grew 17.84% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 17.24% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 0.59pp from 17.2% → 17.8%, the first uptick after a five-period deceleration from 22.6% in Mar 2025.
chain: Medium enterprises credit grew 17.84% YoY in May 2026, up 0.59pp from 17.24% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a five-period slide from 22.6% in Mar 2025 → 17.2% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 17.9%, signaling this segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.
sibc-psl-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises showed a 0.59pp uptick to 17.8%, but it is too early to call a trend reversal — growth remains below the historical p25. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 18% before scaling exposure, as the segment has lost share to Micro & Small throughout the deceleration.

sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.title: PSL MSME 29.4% in FY26 vs 13.4% in FY25 — 15.9pp step-up
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.observation: PSL MSME credit grew 13.4% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 29.4% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 15.9-point acceleration.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth more than doubled from 13.4% in FY25 to 29.4% in FY26, marking the largest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
chain: MSME credit grew 13.4% in FY25 and 29.4% in FY26 — a 15.9-point step-up, the largest in this dataset.
This full-year acceleration reflects the structural breakout in Micro & Small lending, which surged from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 29.4% in Mar 2026.
The 15.9pp gap confirms MSME is the primary driver of PSL expansion, anchoring FY26 performance.
sibc-psl-msme-fy-acceleration.inference: MSME's 15.9pp full-year acceleration from FY25 to FY26 is the structural growth story in PSL. Banks should expect this momentum to anchor FY27 performance — scale origination capacity and monitor credit quality closely as volumes compound at near-30% growth.

sibc-psl-msme-yoy.title: PSL Micro & Small 23.7% YoY — 4.8pp deceleration from 28.5%
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.observation: PSL Micro & Small enterprises credit grew 23.69% year-on-year in May 2026, down from 28.53% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 4.85pp from 28.5% → 23.7%, the sharpest single-period drop since the breakout from 13.4% in Mar 2025.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 23.69% YoY in May 2026, down 4.85pp from 28.53% in Apr 2026.
This is the sharpest deceleration since the surge from 13.4% in Mar 2025 to 24.8% in Jan 2026, though growth remains near the historical median of 28.5%.
The 4.85pp drop suggests the peak growth phase may be moderating, but the structural trend remains intact above 20%.
sibc-psl-msme-yoy.inference: Micro & Small decelerated sharply from 28.5% to 23.7%, but growth remains robust at 23.7% — well above the pre-breakout 13.4%. Banks should monitor whether this stabilises above 20% or decelerates further. The structural trend is intact, but the pace is slowing from the 29.4% peak in Mar 2026.

sibc-psl-others-yoy.title: PSL Others -11.2% YoY — contraction easing from -12.5%
sibc-psl-others-yoy.observation: PSL Others contracted 11.20% year-on-year in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.direction: The contraction narrowed 1.25pp from -12.5% → -11.2%, resuming the easing trend after Apr's reversal from -9.6% in Mar 2026.
chain: PSL Others contracted 11.20% YoY in May 2026, improving from -12.45% in Apr 2026 — a 1.25pp narrowing.

The contraction is now above the historical p75 of -11.8%, signaling the weakest decline in the series.
sibc-psl-others-yoy.inference: PSL Others is stabilising — the move from -12.5% to -11.2% is the weakest contraction on record, above the p75 of -11.8%. Banks should watch whether this turns positive in the next 1–2 periods, signaling a structural recovery in miscellaneous PSL categories.

sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.title: PSL Renewable Energy 12 straight periods of YoY growth — record streak
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.observation: PSL Renewable Energy has posted positive year-on-year growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest streak in this dataset.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.direction: The streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026, with no interruption since the series began in Jan 2025.
chain: Renewable Energy has grown YoY for 12 consecutive periods, extending from 11 in Apr 2026 to 12 in May 2026.
This is the longest positive streak in the dataset, with no interruption since the series began.
The unbroken streak confirms Renewable Energy lending is structurally entrenched in positive territory, supported by policy and ESG tailwinds.
sibc-psl-renewable-pos-streak.inference: Renewable Energy's 12-period positive streak is the longest on record — this is a structurally entrenched opportunity. Banks should expect sustained demand through FY27 and beyond, with policy support and ESG mandates anchoring growth. Scale exposure with confidence.

sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.title: PSL Renewable Energy 17.5% YoY — accelerating from 15.6%
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.observation: PSL Renewable Energy grew 17.46% year-on-year in May 2026, up from 15.64% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 1.82pp from 15.6% → 17.5%, the first uptick after a six-period deceleration from 78.3% in Mar 2025.
chain: Renewable Energy credit grew 17.46% YoY in May 2026, up 1.82pp from 15.64% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration after a six-period slide from 78.3% in Mar 2025 → 15.6% in Apr 2026.
Growth remains below the historical p25 of 21.7%, signaling the segment is still in the lower range despite the uptick.
sibc-psl-renewable-yoy.inference: Renewable Energy accelerated from 15.6% to 17.5%, the first uptick after six periods of deceleration. This may signal stabilisation, but growth remains below the p25 of 21.7%. Banks should monitor whether this holds above 17% for two more periods before scaling exposure — the 12-period positive streak is intact, but the pace is still recovering.

sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.title: PSL Social Infrastructure 70.7% YoY — surging 35.4pp from 35.3%
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.observation: PSL Social Infrastructure grew 70.71% year-on-year in May 2026, surging from 35.27% in Apr 2026.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 35.44pp from 35.3% → 70.7%, the highest reading in the nine-period series and the largest single-period jump on record.
chain: Social Infrastructure credit grew 70.71% YoY in May 2026, up 35.44pp from 35.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the highest reading in the series and the largest single-period jump, after swinging from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 12.9% in Mar 2026 to 35.3% in Apr 2026.
The 35.44pp surge confirms extreme volatility — the segment has swung from negative to 70.7% in just three periods.
sibc-psl-social-infra-yoy.inference: Social Infrastructure surged 35.4pp to 70.7%, the highest on record, but the extreme volatility — from -1.0% in Feb 2026 to 70.7% in May 2026 — makes this segment unpredictable. Banks should treat this as a tactical opportunity, not a structural trend, and avoid scaling exposure until the trajectory stabilises for at least three consecutive periods.

sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.title: PSL Weaker Sections 9.1% YoY — weakest reading on record
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.observation: PSL Weaker Sections (including PSLC SF/MF) grew 9.06% year-on-year in May 2026, the weakest reading in the nine-period series.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 1.61pp from 10.7% → 9.1%, extending a six-period slide from 15.3% in Mar 2025 and now below the historical p25 of 10.9%.
chain: Weaker Sections credit grew 9.06% YoY in May 2026, down 1.61pp from 10.67% in Apr 2026.
This is the weakest reading in the series, below the historical p25 of 10.9%, after a six-period deceleration from 15.3% in Mar 2025.
The segment is losing share to Micro & Small (23.7%) and Housing (39.0%), which are growing 2.6× and 4.3× faster.
sibc-psl-weaker-sections-yoy.inference: Weaker Sections at 9.1% is the weakest on record, losing share to Micro & Small and Housing. This reflects either demand weakness or strategic reallocation by banks toward higher-growth segments. Banks should assess credit quality in this segment — if demand is structurally weak, policy interventions may be needed to revive lending.

sibc-psl-yoy-scan.title: Housing 39.0%, Social Infrastructure 70.7% — PSL spread hits 81.9pp
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.observation: Social Infrastructure grew 70.7% YoY, Housing 39.0%, and Micro and Small Enterprises 23.7% — the three fastest-growing PSL segments.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.direction: The spread between top and bottom performers widened from 52.4pp to 81.9pp. Social Infrastructure doubled from 35.3% to 70.7%. Export Credit improved to -9.7% (from -12.0%), Others to -11.2% (from -12.5%). Five segments shifted status: Housing, Educational Loans, and Agriculture moved to active; Medium Enterprises and Renewable Energy moved to strengthening.
chain: Social Infrastructure surged to 70.7% YoY — doubling from 35.3% last period — while Housing held at 39.0% and Micro and Small Enterprises decelerated to 23.7% from 28.5%.
The 81.9pp spread is the widest on record, driven by Social Infrastructure's breakout acceleration and persistent double-digit declines in Export Credit and Others.
Five status shifts in one period — three segments moving to active, two to strengthening — signal broad-based momentum building across retail and infrastructure PSL categories.
Export Credit's contraction moderated to -9.7% from -12.0%, but remains structurally weak; Others declined -11.2%, also improving from -12.5%.
sibc-psl-yoy-scan.inference: The 81.9pp spread and Social Infrastructure's 70.7% surge confirm PSL is bifurcating into a high-growth retail/infrastructure core and a contracting export/corporate tail. Housing's shift to active at 39.0% and Educational Loans at 22.6% signal sustained momentum in consumer-facing segments. Medium Enterprises and Renewable Energy moving to strengthening status indicate emerging pockets of acceleration. Lenders should overweight Social Infrastructure, Housing, and Education for PSL compliance; Export Credit remains structurally challenged despite moderation. The five simultaneous status shifts mark an inflection — PSL growth is broadening beyond housing into infrastructure and mid-tier enterprise segments.

sector_mix.narrative: Services credit is in a sustained acceleration phase, hitting 20.4% YoY — the highest on record — with 7 of 10 sub-sectors strengthening. Computer Software (54.9%), Shipping (41.0%), and NBFCs (33.7%) are the top growth engines, while Trade credit is re-accelerating within Services: Wholesale at 22.3% is pulling away from Retail at 11.3%, and Wholesale is regaining share dominance at 56.4% of the Trade pool. Lenders should overweight Services exposure, prioritise Wholesale Trade and high-velocity sub-sectors (Software, NBFCs, Shipping), and avoid incremental Retail Trade underwriting until its weakening trend stabilises.

sibc-agriculture-share.title: Agriculture share steady at 12.36% — stable allocation
sibc-agriculture-share.observation: Agriculture's share of total bank credit stands at 12.36% in May 2026.
sibc-agriculture-share.direction: Share declined 0.09pp from 12.44% last period, holding near the median of its nine-period range (12.3%–12.5%).
chain: Agriculture accounts for 12.36% of total bank credit, down 0.09pp from 12.44% last period.
The share has oscillated within a narrow 0.2pp band (12.3%–12.5%) across nine periods, with no sustained directional trend.
This stability indicates agriculture is growing in line with the overall credit pool, neither gaining nor losing structural ground.
sibc-agriculture-share.inference: Agriculture maintains a stable 12.4% footprint in the credit mix — proportional growth signals balanced portfolio expansion. Lenders can sustain current allocation without structural rebalancing pressure.

sibc-agriculture-yoy.title: Agriculture credit at 14.91% YoY — re-accelerating after Apr dip
sibc-agriculture-yoy.observation: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.70% last period.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated 1.21pp from 13.70% to 14.91%, reversing the prior period's 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%. The trajectory shows: 13.7% → 14.9%.
chain: Agriculture credit grew 14.91% YoY in May 2026, up 1.21pp from 13.70% last period.
The prior period saw a 1.92pp deceleration from the Mar 2026 peak of 15.62%; this period reverses that softening.
The current rate of 14.91% sits above the upper range (p75=14.3%), confirming momentum has returned after a brief pause.
sibc-agriculture-yoy.inference: Agriculture lending has resumed its strong trajectory after a one-period dip — the 14.91% rate confirms robust demand, likely driven by Kharif season credit and government-backed schemes. Lenders should maintain exposure; the re-acceleration signals sustained momentum, not a temporary spike.

sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.title: Industry credit grew 8.2% → 15.0% — 6.8pp step-up
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.observation: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, marking a 6.8-point acceleration.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year acceleration, measured at Mar 2026 year-end, reflects a near-doubling of annual growth momentum from the prior fiscal year.
chain: Industry credit grew 8.2% in FY25 and 15.0% in FY26, a 6.8-point step-up.
This acceleration is the largest in the dataset, signaling a structural shift from multi-year slowdown to capex-driven recovery.
The 15.0% FY26 rate is the highest annual growth in recent years, confirming the industrial lending cycle has turned.
sibc-industry-fy-acceleration.inference: Industry credit has broken out of its multi-year slowdown — the 6.8pp acceleration confirms a capex-driven recovery. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, balance sheet repair, and PLI-linked demand. This is the most compelling industrial lending opportunity in years.

sibc-industry-share.title: Industry share at 21.63% — edging up from range low

chain: Industry's share stands at 21.63%, up 0.13pp from 21.50% last period.

Despite strong YoY growth (17.55%), industry is not yet gaining structural share — it is recovering, not reshaping the credit mix.
sibc-industry-share.inference: Industry is in a strong growth phase but not displacing retail or services as the dominant driver. Lenders can lean into industrial exposure for growth, but the sector is not yet commanding a larger slice of the pool. Watch for sustained share gains above 21.9% as a signal of structural rebalancing.

sibc-industry-yoy.title: Industry credit at 17.55% YoY — highest on record
sibc-industry-yoy.observation: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, the fastest rate in this nine-period window.
sibc-industry-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated 2.49pp from 15.06% to 17.55%, breaking above the prior maximum of 15.62%. The trajectory shows sustained acceleration: 15.0% → 15.1% → 17.5%.
chain: Industry credit grew 17.55% YoY in May 2026, up 2.49pp from 15.06% last period.
This is the highest rate in the nine-period window, surpassing the prior peak of 15.62%.
The sustained acceleration from 8.2% in Mar 2025 to 17.55% in May 2026 confirms a capex-driven recovery is entrenched and gaining momentum.
sibc-industry-yoy.inference: Industry lending has reached a new high and is accelerating — the 17.55% pace confirms the capex cycle is strengthening, not plateauing. Lenders should prioritize sectors with strong order books, infrastructure exposure, and balance sheet repair. This is the strongest industrial lending signal in years; any deceleration below 15% would mark a trend break.

sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.title: HFCs 17.7%, PFIs 17.0% — near-parity within NBFC credit

sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.direction: The spread between HFCs and PFIs narrowed from 1.0pp last period to 0.7pp. Both segments remain active.
chain: HFCs hold 17.7% share and PFIs 17.0%, a 0.7pp spread.

PFIs are approaching parity with HFCs within NBFC credit, likely driven by infrastructure or long-tenor project finance.
sibc-nbfc-sub-share-scan.inference: PFIs are closing the gap with HFCs within NBFC credit — the 0.7pp spread is the narrowest in this window. HFCs remain the larger segment, but PFIs are gaining ground. Lenders should assess exposure to both segments as the mix rebalances; a share crossover is possible if PFI growth sustains.

sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.title: PFIs 71.8% YoY, HFCs 19.7% — 52pp spread widens
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Public Financial Institutions grew 71.8% YoY in May 2026, while Housing Finance Companies grew 19.7%.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.direction: PFIs accelerated from 57.1% to 71.8%, widening the spread from 40.4pp to 52.0pp — the largest gap in this scan.
chain: PFIs grew 71.8% YoY, up from 57.1% last period, while HFCs grew 19.7%, up from 16.7%.
The 52.0pp spread is the widest in this window, driven by PFIs sustaining explosive growth.
PFIs are likely benefiting from government-backed infrastructure lending or policy-driven credit expansion, while HFCs are stable but lagging.
sibc-nbfc-sub-yoy-scan.inference: PFIs are sustaining explosive growth — the 71.8% rate and widening spread confirm a structural shift, likely driven by infrastructure or policy-backed lending. HFCs are stable but lagging. Lenders should assess PFI asset quality and concentration risk given the sustained high-velocity expansion; this pace is unsustainable without strong underwriting.

sibc-personal-loans-share.title: Personal loans at 32.81% — stable near range median
sibc-personal-loans-share.observation: Personal loans account for 32.81% of total bank credit in May 2026.

sibc-personal-loans-share.inference: Personal loans have stabilized near 32.8% after a brief bounce last period — the rebalancing toward productive sectors has paused, but retail is not regaining dominance. Lenders should maintain current allocation; the stable share signals balanced portfolio expansion, not a shift back to retail-heavy portfolios.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.title: Personal loans at 15.38% YoY — easing from Apr high
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.observation: Personal loans credit grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down from 16.01% last period.
sibc-personal-loans-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 0.64pp from 16.01% to 15.38%, slipping from near the upper range (p75=15.7%) toward the median. The trajectory shows: 16.0% → 15.4%.
chain: Personal loans grew 15.38% YoY in May 2026, down 0.64pp from 16.01% last period.

sibc-personal-loans-yoy.inference: Personal loans remain a growth engine, sustaining mid-teens expansion, but the deceleration from 16.01% to 15.38% suggests the sector may be plateauing after a strong run. Lenders should monitor unsecured exposure — especially credit cards and personal loans — as growth stabilizes at elevated levels. Watch for further softening below 14% as a signal of peak momentum passing.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.title: Services credit grew 12.0% → 19.0% — 7.0pp step-up
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.observation: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, marking a 7.0-point acceleration.
sibc-services-fy-acceleration.direction: The full-year acceleration measured at Mar 2026 year-end is 7.0pp, the sharpest annual step-up in this dataset.
chain: Services credit grew 12.0% in FY25 and 19.0% in FY26, a 7.0-point step-up.

sibc-services-fy-acceleration.inference: Services credit is in its strongest cycle in recent years — the 7.0-point acceleration confirms a structural shift, not a temporary spike. Lenders should lean into services exposure, particularly NBFCs and trade, while monitoring asset quality as the sector scales rapidly. This is the most compelling services lending opportunity in years.

sibc-services-share.title: Services share at 28.28% — holding upper range
sibc-services-share.observation: Services account for 28.28% of total bank credit in May 2026.

sibc-services-share.inference: Services remain a dominant force in the credit mix, holding near the top of their range. The sector is still capturing a larger slice of the pool than a year ago, but share gains have plateaued. Lenders should maintain services exposure but watch for signs of saturation as share stabilizes around 28.3%.

chain: NBFCs hold 34.5% share, Trade 22.5%, and Other Services 20.2%.

NBFCs remain the dominant force at 34.5%, holding steady. Lenders should monitor Trade for sustained recovery; a return to strengthening status would confirm the turnaround. Maintain NBFC exposure as the anchor segment.

sibc-services-yoy.title: Services credit hit 20.4% YoY — highest on record
sibc-services-yoy.observation: Services credit grew 20.41% year-on-year in May 2026, the strongest reading in the nine-period dataset.
sibc-services-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated from 18.6% in April 2026 to 20.4% in May 2026, reversing the prior period's deceleration. The trajectory over the past year shows: 12.0% → 15.5% → 16.3% → 19.0% → 18.6% → 20.4%.
chain: Services credit grew 20.41% YoY in May 2026, the highest rate in this dataset and 1.82 points above the prior period's 18.58%.
The April deceleration to 18.6% was a false signal — growth has now broken through the March peak of 19.0% and set a new high.
This re-acceleration confirms Services remains the fastest-growing major sector, with momentum building rather than plateauing.
sibc-services-yoy.inference: Services credit is in a sustained acceleration phase — the April dip was noise, not a trend break. Lenders should lean into Services exposure: the 20.4% growth rate is the highest on record and shows no sign of peaking. Prioritise underwriting in high-velocity sub-sectors (Computer Software, NBFCs, Shipping) where growth is compounding. The next period will test whether this is a new plateau or further acceleration — either way, Services is the sector to overweight.

sibc-services-yoy-scan.title: Computer Software 54.9%, Shipping 41.0% — 7 of 10 strengthening
sibc-services-yoy-scan.observation: All 10 services sub-sectors are growing year-on-year. Computer Software leads at 54.9%, followed by Shipping at 41.0% and NBFCs at 33.7%. Seven sub-sectors are strengthening, three are active.

chain: Computer Software at 54.9%, Shipping at 41.0%, and NBFCs at 33.7% are the top three growth engines, all strengthening.

The 47.3-point spread between Computer Software (54.9%) and Transport Operators (7.6%) shows growth is concentrating in technology, logistics, and financial intermediation.
sibc-services-yoy-scan.inference: Services credit growth is broad-based and accelerating — 7 of 10 sub-sectors are strengthening, the widest breadth in this dataset. Lenders should prioritise Computer Software (54.9%), Shipping (41.0%), and NBFCs (33.7%), where momentum is strongest. Aviation (25.8%) and Tourism (22.0%) have re-entered the acceleration phase — both are high-velocity bets for lenders with risk appetite. Transport Operators at 7.6% remains the laggard; avoid incremental exposure until growth stabilises above 10%.

sibc-trade-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Trade credit, Wholesale holds 56.4% share and Retail 44.4%, a 12.8 percentage point spread. Wholesale is strengthening while Retail is weakening.

The 0.8-point share gain for Wholesale and 0.8-point loss for Retail confirm the April shift was temporary. Lenders should prioritise Wholesale exposure — it is the larger segment, growing faster, and now strengthening again. Retail's weakening status and declining share signal demand saturation in consumer-facing commerce; avoid incremental Retail Trade underwriting until the trend stabilises.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.title: Wholesale 22.3%, Retail 11.3% — both re-accelerating
sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Wholesale Trade credit grew 22.3% year-on-year and Retail Trade grew 11.3%, both strengthening. The growth spread between them is 10.9 percentage points.

sibc-trade-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Trade credit growth is re-accelerating across both Wholesale and Retail, with Wholesale maintaining a commanding 22.3% versus Retail's 11.3%. The shift from active to strengthening for both sub-sectors confirms the April deceleration was noise, not a trend break. Lenders should prioritise Wholesale Trade underwriting — it is growing at double Retail's pace and widening the gap. Retail's 11.3% growth is a recovery from the prior period's 8.0% low, but it remains the weaker segment; monitor whether this strengthening persists or fades in the next period.

retail.narrative: Gold loans are reshaping retail credit at record speed: now 7.3% of personal loans (highest on record), growing 105.5% YoY despite decelerating from the 121% peak. Housing has fallen to 48.0% share (lowest on record) and is growing just 10.9% YoY — the weakest in this window. Vehicles at 17.3% YoY and Other PL at 12.5% are the reliable mid-teens growth contributors, both outpacing housing. The personal loan cycle is broadening: six of nine categories are active, two are strengthening (Education 14.0%, Advances against shares 8.3%), and only Consumer durables is contracting. The strategic message is clear: gold is the structural winner, vehicles and other PL are the momentum plays, and housing is losing the battle for incremental capital. Lenders should rebalance portfolios toward gold and vehicles to capture the fastest-growing pockets.

sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.title: Credit cards below 10% YoY for 7 straight periods — record run
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.observation: Credit card growth has remained below 10% YoY for 7 consecutive periods, the longest such run in this dataset.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.direction: The sub-10% streak extended from 6 periods in Apr 2026 to 7 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.

The streak began in Dec 2025 at 1.0% and has persisted through May 2026 at 1.3%, with no period exceeding 3.8%.
This confirms a regime shift from the 10%+ growth rates seen in early 2025 to a sustained low-growth environment.
sibc-pl-cc-below10-streak.inference: The 7-period sub-10% streak is now the defining feature of the card market — this is not a pause, it is a new normal. Lenders should abandon volume-driven strategies and focus on yield maximisation and portfolio quality.

sibc-pl-cc-share.title: Credit card share at 4.20% — lowest in 9-period window

Cards are losing ground structurally within retail credit, not just cyclically.
sibc-pl-cc-share.inference: Credit cards are no longer a growth driver for retail lenders — gold and housing are the new engines. This is a permanent shift toward secured lending. Lenders should reallocate capital and origination capacity accordingly.

sibc-pl-cc-yoy.inference: The Apr 2026 uptick was a false signal — May 2026 confirms the card market remains structurally weak. Lenders should not expect a recovery in FY27. Focus on yield optimisation and risk-adjusted returns, not volume expansion.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.title: Consumer Durables negative for 9 straight periods — record contraction
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.observation: Consumer Durables have posted negative YoY growth for 9 consecutive periods, the longest contraction streak on record.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.direction: The negative streak extended from 8 periods in Apr 2026 to 9 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
chain: Consumer Durables have contracted for 9 consecutive periods, the longest negative run in this dataset.
The streak began in Dec 2025 at -5.1% and has persisted through May 2026 at -2.6%, with no period posting positive growth.
This confirms a structural exit by lenders from durable financing, not a cyclical downturn.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-neg-streak.inference: Consumer durable financing is in terminal decline — lenders have abandoned this segment. Expect continued contraction unless a sharp consumption rebound forces re-entry. No meaningful origination or portfolio growth is visible.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.title: Consumer Durables share at 0.33% — near irrelevance
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.observation: Consumer Durables now represent 0.33% of personal loans, near the median of the 9-period range.
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.direction: Share edged up from 0.32% in Apr 2026 to 0.33% in May 2026, but remains down from 0.4% in early 2025.
chain: Consumer Durables share held at 0.33% in May 2026, near the median of the 9-period range.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-share.inference: Consumer Durables are shrinking into irrelevance within retail credit. Lenders have effectively exited — this segment is no longer material to portfolio strategy or origination planning.

sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.title: Consumer Durables at -2.64% YoY — decline moderating
sibc-pl-consumer-durables-yoy.observation: Consumer Durables outstanding contracted 2.64% YoY in May 2026, an improvement from -3.38% in Apr 2026.

The worst of the contraction was -9.8% in Feb 2026; the May 2026 reading is 7.2 points better.
The moderation suggests the segment is stabilising at a lower base, not recovering.

sibc-pl-education-share.title: Education loans at 2.25% share — lowest in 9-period window
sibc-pl-education-share.observation: Education loans account for 2.25% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window.

chain: Education loan share held at 2.25% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods and down 0.05 points from 2.3% in Jan 2025.
The segment is growing in absolute terms but losing ground relative to faster-moving segments like gold and housing.
This is a stable, low-volatility portfolio with no structural growth catalyst.
sibc-pl-education-share.inference: Education lending is growing but losing share to gold and housing — it is a defensive, low-risk segment, not a growth engine. Expect continued mid-teens growth but no acceleration or share gains.

sibc-pl-education-yoy.title: Education loans at 14.03% YoY — uptick from 13.27%
sibc-pl-education-yoy.observation: Education loans grew 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up from 13.27% in Apr 2026.

chain: Education loan growth rose to 14.03% YoY in May 2026, up 0.76 points from 13.27% in Apr 2026.
This is the first acceleration in four periods, breaking the downward trajectory from 15.9% in Jan 2025.
The uptick suggests stabilisation near the mid-teens level, not a return to the 15%+ rates seen in early 2025.
sibc-pl-education-yoy.inference: Education lending has stabilised near 14% YoY after a gradual deceleration. This is a defensive, low-risk segment with steady mid-teens growth — expect no acceleration but continued stable performance through FY27.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.title: FD advances at 2.21% share — lowest in 9-period window
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.observation: Advances against Fixed Deposits now represent 2.21% of personal loans, the smallest share in this 9-period window.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.direction: Share declined from 2.27% in Apr 2026 to 2.21% in May 2026, continuing the retreat from the 2.5% peak in Dec 2025.
chain: FD advances share fell to 2.21% in May 2026, the lowest reading in 9 periods and down 0.29 points from 2.5% in Dec 2025.
The defensive shift toward FD-backed lending peaked in Dec 2025 and has since reversed.
Lenders are moving away from zero-risk FD-backed products as risk appetite normalises.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-share.inference: The defensive pivot to FD-backed lending is unwinding — lenders are shifting capital back to higher-yielding segments. Expect continued share decline as risk appetite normalises and gold/housing capture incremental flows.

sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.title: FD advances at 8.06% YoY — lowest in 9-period window
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.observation: Advances against Fixed Deposits grew 8.06% YoY in May 2026, the lowest reading in this 9-period series.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated 9.61% → 8.06%, a 1.56-point drop and the weakest rate since this series began in Jan 2025.

The Mar 2026 spike to 19.22% was a one-time event — the May 2026 reading confirms a return to single-digit growth.
The deceleration suggests lenders are de-prioritising this zero-risk product as risk appetite normalises.
sibc-pl-fd-advances-yoy.inference: The Mar 2026 spike in FD-backed lending was an anomaly — May 2026 confirms a return to single-digit growth. Lenders are shifting capital to higher-yielding segments. Expect continued deceleration as the defensive pivot unwinds.

sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.title: Gold loans positive for 12 straight periods — record expansion
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.observation: Gold loans have posted positive YoY growth for 12 consecutive periods, the longest unbroken expansion streak in this dataset.
sibc-pl-gold-pos-streak.direction: The positive streak extended from 11 periods in Apr 2026 to 12 periods in May 2026, a new historical maximum.
chain: Gold loans have grown every period for 12 consecutive months, the longest run on record.

industry.narrative: Industry credit is undergoing a structural reallocation from large corporates to MSME borrowers. Large corporates re-accelerated to 14.4% YoY in May 2026 — the strongest pace in 16 months — but their share has stabilised at 67.3% after losing 3.7pp since Jan 2025. Micro & Small enterprises grew 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25, a 23.8pp step-up that marks the defining inflection in this dataset. However, Micro & Small growth has decelerated to 26.2% YoY in May 2026 from a 32.7% peak in Mar 2026, and the MSME-to-large-corporate growth spread has narrowed sharply from 20.1pp to 11.8pp. The divergence is compressing as large corporates re-accelerate and MSME decelerates, but MSME segments still capture the bulk of incremental credit. Within Textiles, Other Textiles is gaining share and momentum at 17.1% YoY, while Cotton Textiles is the weakest link at 3.2% YoY despite holding 35.6% of the pool. Lenders overweight to large corporates are capturing more incremental credit than before, but the structural advantage remains with MSME borrowers — particularly Micro & Small, which hold 23.1% of industry credit and are growing at 1.8× the pace of large corporates.

With all 19 types active for a second period, industry credit is in equilibrium — growth is broad-based but no segment is breaking out. Maintain overweight Infrastructure exposure and balanced allocations across the top five segments (Infrastructure, Basic Metal, Other Industries, All Engineering, Chemicals). Watch for status shifts in the next period to identify emerging concentration or diversification trends.

sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.title: Petroleum surges 40.9% YoY — 16 of 19 types strengthening
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.observation: Petroleum, Coal Products and Nuclear Fuels grew 40.9% year-on-year, Gems and Jewellery 35.5%, and All Engineering 35.1%. Leather and Leather Products grew just 8.8%.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.direction: Petroleum accelerated from 37.8% → 40.9%, sustaining strengthening status. Gems and Jewellery accelerated from 26.0% → 35.5%, flipping from active to strengthening. All Engineering accelerated from 30.8% → 35.1%, flipping from active to strengthening. Sixteen of 19 segments are now strengthening, up from ten last period. The spread is 32.0pp, up from 31.3pp.
chain: Petroleum grew 40.9% year-on-year, accelerating 3.1pp from 37.8% and sustaining strengthening status — the highest growth rate across all 19 industry types.
Gems and Jewellery accelerated 9.5pp from 26.0% to 35.5%, flipping from active to strengthening and reversing last period's sharp deceleration from 41.4%.
All Engineering accelerated 4.3pp from 30.8% to 35.1%, flipping from active to strengthening and confirming the capex boom is re-accelerating.
Sixteen of 19 segments are now strengthening, up from ten last period, signaling a sharp return to broad-based momentum across industry credit.
sibc-industry-type-yoy-scan.inference: Petroleum's 40.9% growth and sustained strengthening status confirm it is the growth leader — driven by energy transition capex, refining capacity expansion, and petrochemical demand. Prioritise exposure here. Gems and Jewellery's 9.5pp acceleration and flip to strengthening reverse last period's sharp deceleration — export demand has recovered or working capital cycles have normalised. All Engineering's flip to strengthening confirms the capex boom is re-accelerating. The jump from ten to sixteen strengthening segments signals a sharp return to broad-based momentum — the prior period's concentration warning is now reversed. Maintain overweight exposure to Petroleum, Gems, and Engineering.

sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.title: Power rises to 57.0% of Infrastructure — Telecom stable at 6.5%
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Infrastructure, Power holds 57.0%, Roads 22.6%, and Other Infrastructure 12.2%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) accounts for just 0.5%.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.direction: Power's share rose from 56.8% → 57.0%. Telecommunications held steady at 6.5%, shifting from weakening to active status. The spread is 56.6pp, up from 56.4pp.
chain: Power's share rose 0.2pp to 57.0%, extending its dominance within Infrastructure for a third consecutive period.
Telecommunications held steady at 6.5%, flipping from weakening to active status after falling from 6.6% last period.
The spread widened marginally from 56.4pp to 56.6pp, confirming Power's continued concentration within Infrastructure credit.
Telecom's status flip to active signals the prior period's share loss has stabilised, but it remains a small segment with no growth momentum.
sibc-infra-sub-share-scan.inference: Power's 0.2pp gain and 57.0% share confirm sustained investment in renewable energy and thermal capacity — maintain overweight exposure. Telecom's flip to active status signals the prior period's weakening has stabilised, but at 6.5% it remains a small segment with no strategic relevance. Roads at 22.6% remains the second-largest segment but is not gaining ground — growth is concentrating in Power. With Power holding above 57%, Infrastructure credit is increasingly a Power-led story.

sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.title: Ports surges 82.8% YoY — Railways contracts -37.6%
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Ports grew 82.8% year-on-year, Power 23.8%, and Other Infrastructure 1.5%. Railways (other than Indian Railways) contracted -37.6%, Airports -14.5%, and Telecommunications -7.0%.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Ports accelerated from 74.0% → 82.8%, sustaining strengthening status. Power accelerated from 21.5% → 23.8%, flipping from active to strengthening. Roads reversed from -0.7% growth to -0.7% contraction, flipping from active to declining. The spread is 120.4pp, up from 118.1pp.
chain: Ports grew 82.8% year-on-year, accelerating 8.8pp from 74.0% and sustaining strengthening status — the highest growth rate within Infrastructure.
Power grew 23.8%, accelerating 2.3pp from 21.5% and flipping from active to strengthening, confirming sustained investment in renewable energy and thermal capacity.
Roads reversed from -0.7% growth to -0.7% contraction, flipping from active to declining and signaling a sharp turn in road infrastructure credit.
The spread widened from 118.1pp to 120.4pp — the widest in this domain — with four of seven entities in contraction.
sibc-infra-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Ports' 82.8% growth and sustained strengthening status confirm it is the standout growth driver within Infrastructure — driven by trade volume recovery, capacity expansion, and logistics capex. Prioritise exposure here. Power's 23.8% growth and flip to strengthening confirm robust demand in renewable energy and thermal capacity. Roads' flip from growth to -0.7% contraction is the key warning signal — project delays, refinancing stress, or lender exits. Avoid new Roads commitments until growth stabilises above zero. The 120.4pp spread confirms extreme polarisation: Power and Ports are surging while Roads, Telecom, Airports, and Railways collapse.

sibc-large-corporate-share.title: Large corporates hold 67.3% of industry credit — share stable
sibc-large-corporate-share.observation: Large corporates account for 67.3% of industry credit in May 2026, up 0.11pp from the prior period.
sibc-large-corporate-share.direction: Share compressed from 71.0% in Jan 2025 to 67.3% in May 2026, losing 3.7 percentage points over 16 months.
chain: Large corporates hold 67.3% of industry credit in May 2026, down 3.7pp from 71.0% in Jan 2025.
The share has stabilised near 67.2% for the past three periods after a sharp 3.8pp decline through early 2026.
The structural shift is complete — large corporates have ceded share to MSME segments, and the new equilibrium is holding.
sibc-large-corporate-share.inference: Large corporates have lost 3.7pp of share but the decline has plateaued. The segment is no longer bleeding share, but it is not reclaiming lost ground either. Lenders overweight to large corporates are capturing a smaller slice of incremental industry credit — the growth opportunity has shifted to MSME borrowers.

sibc-large-corporate-yoy.title: Large corporate credit at 14.4% YoY — strongest in 16 months
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.observation: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY in May 2026, the strongest reading in this nine-period window.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.direction: Growth accelerated from 10.0% in Apr 2026 to 14.4%, extending a recovery that began at 5.5% in Jan 2026.
chain: Large corporate credit grew 14.4% YoY in May 2026, up 4.34pp from 10.0% last period.
This is the fastest pace in 16 months and marks a sharp re-acceleration after six periods of single-digit growth.
Despite the 14.4% pace, large corporates are still losing share — MSME segments are growing even faster.
sibc-large-corporate-yoy.inference: Large corporates are re-accelerating hard, but the 14.4% pace is insufficient to defend market share against 26%+ MSME growth. The segment is healthy and momentum is building, but lenders here are capturing a minority of incremental industry credit. The growth is real, but the structural advantage lies elsewhere.

sibc-msme-medium-share.title: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% share — four-period plateau continues
sibc-msme-medium-share.observation: Medium enterprises account for 9.7% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.05pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-medium-share.direction: Share rose from 8.9% in Jan 2025 to 9.7% by Jan 2026 and has held at 9.7% for five consecutive periods.
chain: Medium enterprises hold 9.7% of industry credit in May 2026, unchanged for five straight periods.
The segment gained 0.8pp of share from Jan 2025 to Jan 2026, then stabilised.
The five-period plateau signals the segment is growing in line with the broader industry pool, not outpacing it.
sibc-msme-medium-share.inference: Medium enterprises have carved out a 9.7% share and are defending it. The five-period plateau confirms the segment is capturing steady flow, not outsized growth. Lenders here are riding industry-level growth, not a structural advantage.

sibc-msme-medium-yoy.title: Medium enterprises at 21.2% YoY — momentum stabilising
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.observation: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY in May 2026, up 1.29pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.direction: Growth re-accelerated from 19.9% in Apr 2026 to 21.2%, after decelerating from a 22.3% peak in Jan 2026.
chain: Medium enterprise credit grew 21.2% YoY in May 2026, up 1.29pp from 19.9% last period.
The segment had decelerated from 22.3% in Jan 2026 to 19.9% in Apr 2026, but has now stabilised above 21%.
The 21.2% pace is healthy and far ahead of large corporates at 14.4%, but the segment is no longer accelerating.
sibc-msme-medium-yoy.inference: Medium enterprises have stabilised at a strong 21% growth rate after a brief deceleration. The segment remains a solid outperformer, but the momentum is no longer building. Lenders should watch for further deceleration — if growth drops below 19%, the segment risks losing share to Micro & Small borrowers.

sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.title: Micro & Small grew 32.7% in FY26 vs 8.9% in FY25 — 23.8pp step-up
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in the year ending Mar 2025 and 32.7% in the year ending Mar 2026, a 23.8 percentage point acceleration.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.direction: Full-year growth jumped from 8.9% in FY25 to 32.7% in FY26 — the sharpest year-over-year step-up in this dataset.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 8.9% in FY25 and 32.7% in FY26, a 23.8pp acceleration.
This is the largest full-year step-up on record, signalling a regime shift in lending to smaller borrowers.
The 32.7% FY26 pace is 3.7× the FY25 rate — this is not a cyclical uptick, it is a structural inflection.
sibc-msme-micro-small-fy-acceleration.inference: This is the defining inflection in industry credit. Micro & Small lending has entered a new growth regime, and lenders with exposure here are riding the fastest-growing segment. The 23.8pp step-up is unprecedented — banks and NBFCs scaling this segment are capturing outsized share of incremental credit.

sibc-msme-micro-small-share.title: Micro & Small hold 23.1% of industry credit — share stable
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.observation: Micro & Small enterprises account for 23.1% of industry credit in May 2026, down 0.06pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.direction: Share surged from 20.0% in Mar 2025 to 23.5% in Jan 2026, then eased to 23.1% — a net gain of 3.1 percentage points in 14 months.
chain: Micro & Small enterprises hold 23.1% of industry credit in May 2026, stable for four straight periods.
The segment gained 3.1pp of share from Mar 2025 to Jan 2026, then plateaued.
The four-period plateau at 23.1% signals the segment is growing in line with the broader pool, not outpacing it anymore.
sibc-msme-micro-small-share.inference: Micro & Small borrowers captured 3.1pp of share in 14 months — a structural reallocation from large corporates. The share has now stabilised at 23.1%, suggesting the segment is no longer gaining ground. Lenders focused on large corporates have lost share, but the incremental opportunity in Micro & Small may be moderating.

sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.title: Micro & Small at 26.2% YoY — decelerating from 32.7% peak
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.observation: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026, down 3.91pp from the prior period.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.direction: Growth decelerated from 30.1% in Apr 2026 to 26.2%, after peaking at 32.7% in Mar 2026.
chain: Micro & Small credit grew 26.2% YoY in May 2026, down 3.91pp from 30.1% last period.
The segment has decelerated for two straight periods after holding above 30% for four periods.
Despite the deceleration, the 26.2% pace is still 1.8× the 14.4% pace of large corporates.
sibc-msme-micro-small-yoy.inference: Micro & Small credit is decelerating but remains the hottest segment in industry credit. The 26.2% pace is still strong, but the two-period decline from a 32.7% peak signals momentum is softening. Lenders should watch closely — if growth drops below 25%, the segment risks losing its structural advantage.

sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.title: MSME growing 11.8pp faster than large corporates — spread narrowing
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.observation: MSME credit is growing 11.8 percentage points faster than large corporate credit in May 2026.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.direction: The spread narrowed from 20.1pp in Apr 2026 to 11.8pp, after widening from 2.1pp in Mar 2025 to 25.7pp in Jan 2026.
chain: MSME credit is growing 11.8pp faster than large corporate credit in May 2026, down 8.3pp from 20.1pp last period.
The spread has narrowed sharply because large corporates re-accelerated to 14.4% while MSME decelerated to 26.2%.
Despite the narrowing, the 11.8pp spread confirms MSME segments are still capturing the bulk of incremental credit.
sibc-msme-size-yoy-spread.inference: The growth divergence between MSME and large corporates is narrowing fast as large corporates re-accelerate and MSME decelerates. The 11.8pp spread is the narrowest since Mar 2025, but MSME segments are still outpacing large corporates by a wide margin. Lenders overweight to large corporates are capturing more incremental credit than before, but the structural advantage remains with MSME borrowers.

sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.title: Other Textiles at 45.3% share — gaining ground vs Cotton at 35.6%
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Other Textiles accounts for 45.3% of credit, Cotton Textiles 35.6%, Man-Made 17.3%, and Jute 1.7%.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.direction: Other Textiles gained share from 44.7% last period to 45.3%, while Cotton Textiles lost share from 36.5% to 35.6%. Man-Made and Jute held steady.
chain: Other Textiles holds 45.3% of Textiles credit in May 2026, up 0.6pp from 44.7% last period.
Cotton Textiles lost 0.9pp of share, falling from 36.5% to 35.6%.
Other Textiles shifted from active to strengthening status, while Cotton shifted from active to weakening — a clear divergence in momentum.
sibc-textiles-sub-share-scan.inference: Other Textiles is gaining share at the expense of Cotton Textiles. The 0.6pp gain for Other and 0.9pp loss for Cotton signals a structural shift within the sector. Lenders with exposure to Cotton are backing the weakening segment — the incremental credit opportunity is in Other Textiles, which now holds 45.3% of the pool and is strengthening.

sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.title: Jute at 20.2% YoY leads Textiles — Other at 17.1%, Cotton at 3.2%
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.observation: Within Textiles, Jute credit grew 20.2% YoY, Other Textiles 17.1%, Man-Made 8.1%, and Cotton Textiles 3.2%.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.direction: Jute leads at 20.2%, followed by Other Textiles at 17.1%. Cotton Textiles is the weakest at 3.2%, while Man-Made is at 8.1%. The spread across the four segments is 17.0 percentage points.
chain: Jute credit grew 20.2% YoY in May 2026, the fastest pace among Textiles sub-segments.
Other Textiles grew 17.1%, Man-Made 8.1%, and Cotton Textiles 3.2% — a 17.0pp spread.
Cotton Textiles shifted from strengthening to active status, while Other and Man-Made shifted from active to strengthening — momentum is concentrating in Jute, Other, and Man-Made.
sibc-textiles-sub-yoy-scan.inference: Textiles credit is in a broad-based recovery, but Cotton Textiles is the laggard. At 3.2% YoY, Cotton is growing at one-sixth the pace of Jute and one-fifth the pace of Other. Cotton holds 35.6% of the pool but is losing share and momentum — lenders with exposure here are backing the weakest link in the recovery. The growth opportunity is in Jute and Other Textiles.
